Details:
Similar to other advanced oxidation technologies that create hydroxyl radicals, ultraviolet irradiation combined with hydrogen peroxide was found to be effective at removing 1,4-dioxane (up to greater than 99 percent removal). Data suggest that the removal efficiency depends on UV dose, hydrogen peroxide dose, contact time, 1,4-dioxane concentration, and hydroxyl radical scavenger (e.g., bicarbonate alkalinity). Many early studies treated water with high 1,4-dioxane concentrations (4 to 450 milligrams per liter) and/or employed hydrogen peroxide doses and UV reaction times greater than typical of drinking water treatment [2033, 2034, 2046, 2075, 2085, 2108]. More recent studies, however, have achieved high removals (up to greater than 99 percent) using more typical hydrogen peroxide doses and UV reaction times [2479, 2480, 2481, 2482, 2721]. These studies include those treating water with low 1,4-dioxane concentrations (4 to 20 micrograms per liter) [2479, 2481, 2723, 2725, 2727, 2730]. 
 Fenton and ferrioxalate were reported to improve 1,4-dioxane degradation rate by UV and hydrogen peroxide. These studies employed relatively high iron doses (30 milligrams per liter and greater) [2046,2085].
